hero.subtitle: The threat to your Bitcoin is not cryptographic. It is physical.

wrench.aria: Two-panel illustration: Panel 1 shows a figure protected by cryptographic defenses. Panel 2 shows the same figure confronted by someone with a wrench.

threatModel.title: Who are you protecting against?
threatModel.opportunistBody: Does not know how much you hold. Targets of opportunity. Standard security (hardware wallet, safe, discretion) is sufficient. The new self-custody declaration law is a direct attack against this layer.
threatModel.targetedBody: Knows you hold significant value (from a data leak, a public declaration, social engineering). Patient. May return. Standard security fails here. Time and distribution are your only real tools.
threatModel.coerciveBody: Has leverage (family, duration, escalation). No architecture eliminates this entirely. The goal is not invulnerability — it is making the attack too costly, too slow, and too uncertain to be worth it.
threatModel.disclaimer: No architecture guarantees absolute protection. The goal is asymmetric cost: the attack must cost more than the expected gain.

crypto.title: The primitives that governments and institutions use — are now accessible to you.
crypto.p1: For decades, advanced cryptographic architectures — secret distribution, threshold signatures, time-delayed access — were reserved for state actors and large financial institutions.
crypto.p2: Bitcoin and its surrounding tooling have made these primitives accessible to any holder willing to implement them correctly. The gap between a government and an individual is no longer the cryptography available — it is the implementation, the operational security, and the architecture built around it.
crypto.quoteLine1: "If someone captures the President, they don't get the nuclear codes.
crypto.quoteAfter: The same architectural principle applies to your Bitcoin.

sss.sectionTitle: See it work. Understand why it matters.
sss.sectionIntro: Shamir's Secret Sharing splits a secret into N shares, requiring M of them to reconstruct it. No single share reveals anything about the secret.
sss.placeholderSecret: e.g. a seed phrase, a password, any string
sss.bp1: Never store all shares in the same location or medium
sss.bp2: Never store shares digitally if they protect a seed phrase
sss.bp3: One share at home, one with a trusted third party, one in a geographically separate location — never a close relative who could be identified
sss.bp4: The reconstruction moment is your most vulnerable moment — prepare the environment accordingly
sss.bp5: A share alone reveals nothing. Protect the schema (how many, what threshold), not just the shares.
sss.bp6: Test your reconstruction before you need it
sss.disclaimer: This demo runs entirely in your browser. Nothing is transmitted. Use for educational purposes. For production implementations protecting significant value, consult a specialist.
sss.errEmptySecret: Please enter a secret to split.
sss.errSplitFailed: Failed to split the secret. Please check your inputs.

shareDiagram.aria: Diagram showing {{total}} shares, {{required}} required to reconstruct
shareDiagram.caption: {{required}} of {{total}} shares needed to reconstruct

above.title: SSS is a strong foundation. There are levels above.
above.p1: SSS has one structural limitation: the secret must be reconstructed at some point, creating a moment of exposure. During reconstruction, the full secret exists in one place — however briefly.
above.p2: Institutional environments use architectures where the secret is never reconstructed in full. Distributed computation allows signing without any single party holding the complete key. These approaches — used by major custody providers and financial institutions — are becoming accessible but require careful, contextual implementation.
above.p3: The right architecture depends on your threat model, your holdings, and your operational constraints. Here is what it looks like when you build it seriously.

institutional.title: Institutional Cold Storage. What the architecture actually looks like.
institutional.intro:
A 3-of-5 multisig across hardware wallets, with geographically distributed encrypted backups and policy validation before any signing occurs. Each layer exists to make a different class of attack economically irrational, logistically impossible, or both.

This is not a product recommendation. It is a map of the decisions.
institutional.stackLabel: The stack. Four layers, four failure modes.
institutional.hardwareSub:
5 × Coldcard (air-gapped)
No single device holds more than one key
institutional.backupSub:
SLIP-39 shares
Geographic distribution
3-of-5 to reconstruct
institutional.scriptSub:
P2WSH multisig 3-of-5
Keys hidden until spend
institutional.policySub:
Schema validation before signing
Declarative, auditable
institutional.workflowTitle: Every spend is a physical process.
institutional.workflowBody:
A transaction is constructed offline. It travels on a microSD card to the first signing device, then the second, then the third. No single machine ever holds more than one key. No key ever touches a networked device.

Before the first signing device sees the transaction, a declarative schema validates that the outputs comply with predefined policy — correct destination, correct script type, fee rate within bounds. Not just cryptographically valid. Politically valid, against rules defined in advance by all parties.

The friction is the architecture. Slowing down a spend is not a bug. It is the mechanism by which coercion becomes logistically expensive.
institutional.btslNote: Policy validation at this layer is what BTSL addresses — a declarative schema all five participants can verify independently before signing.
institutional.card1Limit: Every spend requires physical coordination between signing devices. For funds that are never moved, this is ideal. For an active treasury, it is unworkable — hence the necessity of a separate hot wallet for current operations.
institutional.card1Question: If three of your signers need to coordinate on short notice — what is your out-of-band communication channel?
institutional.card2Limit: Seed reconstruction creates a window of exposure. For a brief moment, the complete secret exists in RAM on a single device. That device must be air-gapped. That moment must be documented and tested before you need it.
institutional.card2Question: Has your designated heir performed a recovery test in the last twelve months? Who is responsible for scheduling it?
institutional.card3Limit: No key length and no hardware wallet changes the calculus against a determined physical adversary with direct access to your person. Geographic distribution of signing authority raises the cost of simultaneous coercion — it does not eliminate it.
institutional.card3Question: If three of your co-signers are approached simultaneously — who finds out first?
institutional.card4Limit: Public keys exposed on-chain in legacy formats carry a long-horizon risk. BIP-360 is in active specification. Migration from a vulnerable architecture requires planning before urgency forces the decision.
institutional.card4Question: Is your current setup migratable without a full custody reconstruction? Do you know?
institutional.cardsDisclaimer: No architecture eliminates these questions. The goal is to have answered them before an event forces the answer.
institutional.ctaText:
Designing this architecture takes 3 to 6 months.
Auditing an existing setup takes 2 to 4 weeks.
Both are available.

consultation.title: Your situation is unique. Your architecture should be too.
consultation.intro: A recommendation without context is a bad recommendation. I offer tailored security architecture consultations for Bitcoin holders — from threat model assessment to implementation guidance.
consultation.tier1Desc: I define who you're protecting against and map your current exposure.
consultation.tier2Desc: A written, personalized document detailing the architecture adapted to your profile and holdings. Implementation remains your responsibility.
consultation.tier3Desc: Step-by-step guidance as you implement. I never touch your keys. Ever.
consultation.disclaimer: All services are advisory in nature. Implementation decisions and operational responsibility remain solely with the client. I hold no access to client funds or secrets.
consultation.formSuccess: Message sent. I'll get back to you shortly.
